text: DUSSAC, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Dordogne, cant. of La Nouaille. Pop. 1,005.
DUSSARA, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Gujerat, in N lat. 23° 16'. It is fortified, and contains about 1,300 houses. The surrounding country is well-cultivated.
DUSSEI, or Duswei, a river of Assam, descending from the Naga mountains, and flowing through the prov. of Korungh, to the Dehingh, which it joins a little above Kuntiaputta.

DUSSELDORF, a regency and a circle of Prussia, in the prov. of the Rhine, comprising the N portion of the duchy of Berg, with the ci-devant bishoprics of Essen and Werder, a part of the archbishopric of Cologne, and of the duchy of Juliers, and the lordships of Broick and Stirum. Its length from E to W is 54 m.; its breadth from N to S 39 m.; and its superficial area 2,064 sq. m. The Rhine, which runs through it from N to S, divides it into nearly equal parts, and is joined by several small streams, amongst which are the Wipper, the Ruhr, and the Erst. The soil in the neighbourhood of the Rhine is partially level and fertile; towards the E it is mountainous and woody. The extent of arable and 680,000 acres, and 155,000 acres; the woods and forests at 303,000 acres. The mineral productions are iron, copper, lead, and mercury. The principal manufactures are linen, cloth, and cotton and silk stuffs, thread, leather, steel, iron ware, and cutlery.— It is divided into 12 circles, viz.: Geldern, Kempen, Crefeld, D., Elberfeld, Gladbach, Grevenbraich, Lennep, Mettman, Neuss, Opladen, and Sollingen. It comprises 30 towns, 9 boroughs, and 4,500 hamlets. Pop. in 1816, 577,000; in 1837, 766,837, of whom 6,500 are Jews; the rest are Catholics and Protestants in nearly equal numbers. Dusseldorf, the capital of the above prov., is situated on the r. bank of the Rhine, at its confluence with the Dussel, in N lat. 51° 13' 42", E long. 6° 46' 10", 21 m. NNW of Cologne, and 16 m. W of Elberfeld. It was originally a strongly fortified town; but in 1801 the fortifications were razed, and extensive promenades formed upon their site. It consists of 3 divisions: the Alstadt, Neustadt, and Kartstadt. The streets and squares are large and regular; and the houses well-built. The most remarkable square is the old market-place, which is ornamented with an equestrian statue of the elector-palatine John Wil- The government-house and town-hall are liam. fine buildings. D. ranks amongst the handsomest towns in Germany. It contains numerous churches, a synagogue, a seminary, 2 hospitals, an orphanasylum, a theatre, mint, and extensive barracks. An academy of sciences, originally founded at Driesbourg, was transferred to this town in 1806. It has also a Catholic gymnasium with 14 professors, an observatory, an academy of painting and design, an infirmary, a library, and a museum. The castle, which was greatly damaged by the bombardment of 1794, was subsequently repaired; and now contains a gallery of paintings by the most eminent masters. -The principal manufactures in D. are those of cloth, cashmere, and tobacco. It has several cottonprinting establishments, spinning-mills, and tanneries; and is the principal seat of commerce between Holland, Germany, and Switzerland. It is a free port, and its warehouses are open to the produce of different countries. It is connected by railway with Cologne and Minden on the one hand; and with Elberfeld on the other. In 1806, D. became the residence of the grand duke of Berg, and the seat of his government. In 1815 it was ceded to Prussia with the duchy of Berg.—D. forms two circles; one comprehending the town; the other the territory of D., which includes 3 cities, and 342 villages or hamlets. Pop. in 1837, 21,858; in 1845, 23,517.
DUSSLINGEN, a village of Wurtemberg, in the circle of the Black forest, 5 m. S of Tubingen. Pop. 2,287.

DUST, or Dusti, a river of Beluchistan, flowing from the interior, through Mekran, into the Indian ocean, in N lat. 25° 3'. At low water it is only from 10 to 30 yds. broad, but with the rise of the tide it assumes the appearance of a fine river. The D. is called Muledani on passing Kej; and receives various names in different parts of its course. Pottinger supposes that the course of this river may be traced 1,000 m. under different appellations.

DUSTON, a parish in Northamptonshire, 2 m. W of Northampton. Area 1,760 acres. Pop. 687. of New York, U.S. It lies on the E side of Hudson's river, 65 m. N of New York. Area 765 sq. m. The N part of this co. is mountainous; the E hilly with occasional lofty summits; the remainder presents a surface much broken. Pop. 52,398.

DUTHILL, a parish of Scotland, in Invernessshire, 20 m. SE of Inverness, united with Rothiemurchus in Moray. The river Spey runs between DVI the two districts; and the Dulnan intersects D. for 13 m. Pop. in 1801, 1,578; in 1841, 1,769. The great pine forest of Rothiemurchus is supposed to cover an extent of 14 or 16 sq. m.
DUTI, or DEPAL, a town of Hindostan, on the Setigunga river, 85 m. NE of Bareilly.
DUTKINA, a town of Siberia, in the gov. of Irkutsk, on the river Lena, 16 m. S of Orlenga.
DUTTAR, a town and district of India, in the prov. of Lahore, 60 m. E of Amritsir.

DUTTEAH, a town and rajaship in N. India, in the Bundlecund territory, 50 m. SSE of Gwalior. The town is remarkably clean and neat, and is encompassed with a strong wall, and defended by a strong citadel. A broad belt of tall tree jungle surrounds the town; and at its W end is an old palace of great extent, and very solidly built.
DUTTLENHEIM, a village of France, in the dep. of Bas-Rhin, cant. of Geispolheim. Pop. 1,270.

DUTTON, a township in the p. of Great Budworth, Cheshire, 5 m. E of Frodsham, on the E bank of the Weaver, in the vicinity of the Grand Junction railway, and close upon the Grand Trunk canal. Area 1,760 acres. Pop. in 1801, 301; in 1841, 361. The Grand Junction railway viaduct across the valley of the Weaver, near this township, a gigantic structure of red stone, procured from the neighbourhood of Bolton and Runcorn, consists of 20 arches, each of 60 ft. span and 60 ft. in height; the battlements add 12 ft. more to the height; the whole length is 1,400 ft. Nearly 700,000 sq. ft. of stone were used in this work, which was finished at an expense of £50,000.—D. gives the title of baron to the duke of Hamilton.—Also a township in the p. of Ribchester, Lancashire, 6½ m. N by W of Blackburn. Pop. in 1801, 388; in 1841, 563.
DUTTON-CACCA, a township in the p. of Holt, Denbighshire, 5\frac{1}{2} m. NE of Wrexham, on the river Dee. Pop. in 1801, 99; in 1841, 110.
DUTTON-DRIFFITH, a township in the p. of Holt, Denbighshire, 5 m. NE of Wrexham, on the W bank of the Dee. Pop. in 1801, 103; in 1841, 179.
DUTTWEILER, a village of Prussia, in the prov. of the Rhine, circle and 2 m. N of Saarbruck, on the Fischbach. Pop. 1,400. Vitriol and alum are manufactured here.
DUTURNA, a village of Sinde, 27 m. NE of Larkhana.
DUVAL, a county in the NE of the state of Florida, U. S. Area 720 sq. m. Pop. 4,156.
DUX, or Duchcow, a small town of Bohemia, in the circle of Leitmeritz, 15 m. WNW of Leitmeritz, at the bottom of a steep hill.
DUXBURY, a township in the p. of Standish, in Lancashire, 2 m. S of Chorley, intersected by the Wigan and Preston railway. Area 1,090 acres. Pop. in 1801, 255; in 1841, 371.
DUXBURY, a township in the state of Massachusetts, U. S., 30 m. SSE of Boston. Pop. 2,798.— Also a township in Vermont, 13 m. W of Montpelier. Pop. 820.
DUXFORD, a parish in the co. of Cambridge, 5\frac{1}{2} m. W of Linton, on the W bank of the Cam. Area 2,982 acres. Pop. in 1801, 464; in 1841, 763.
DUYVELAND. See DUIVELAND. DVINA, DUNA, or NORTHERN DWINA, a river of

DUTCHESS, a county in the SE part of the state | Russia in Europe, formed by the junction of two navigable rivers, the Soukhona and Jong, which descend from a chain of hills running between the Scandinavian Alps and the Ural mountains, and forming the N side of the great basin of the Volga. These rivers unite in the gov. of Vologda, a little below Velikioustioug; and their united stream first directs its course in a N direction to the confluence of the Solvytchegodsk; thence it bends NW, and passes Krasnoborsk; and crossing the gov. of Archangel, in which it waters Kholmogori and Archangel, throws itself, by several mouths, into the gulf of the White sea, which bears its name. The total course of this river—one of the largest in Russia—is 420 m.; and its greatest breadth 5 m. Its depth is considerable; but its navigation is impeded by beds of mud, which bar its embouchures, and by the number of islands with which, throughout the greater extent of its course, its channel is obstructed. The tides extend to the distance of 30 m. above Archangel. Of its numerous affluents the principal on the r. bank are: the Vytchegda, the Ouvtiougha, the two Toïma, the Vaengha, Pinghicha, Poukchenga, Pinega, and Lodma; and on the l. the Oustionmej, the Kodima, Vaga, Emtsa, and Laïa. The D. was for a long time the only outlet for the productions of European Russia. The country through which it passes is low and level, and is to a great extent laid under water by its inundations in the spring. Connections have been formed between this river and the Volga, by means of canals; one of which joins the Keltma, one of the head streams of the Vytchegda, with the Kama; and the other, known as the Lubinski canal, unites the Sukhoma with the Neva by means of the Cheksna.
DVINVILIPSKOE, a lake of Russia, in the gov. of Pskov, district of Toropetz, about 12 m. in length by 3 m. in breadth.
DVOR (NovoI), a town of Russia, in the gov. and 20 m. E of Grodno.
DVORETZ, a town of Russia, in the gov. and 80 m. ESE of Grodno.
DVOULOUTSCHNAIA, a town of Russia, in the gov. and 150 m. SSW of Voronetz, on the l. bank of the Oskol.
DVOURIETCHNAIA, a town of Russia, in the gov. and 70 m. E of Kharkov, on a stream of the same name, an affluent of the Oskol.

DWARACA, a town and celebrated temple of Hindostan, in the prov. of Gujerat, at the SW extremity of the peninsula, in N lat. 22° 15'. The town consists of about 2,560 houses; and there are 21 villages dependent on it. The inhabitants were formerly much addicted to piracy, but have been restrained by a treaty with the British. The temple is fabled to have been the residence of Krishna, at whose shrine here 15,000 pilgrims annually pay their devotions.
DWIGHT, a village in Pope co., in the state of Arkansas, 77 m. NW of Little Rock.
DWINA. See DUNA.
DWYGYFYLCHI, a parish in the co. of Carnarvon, 2½ m. W of Conwy, on the coast. Pop. in 1801, 281; in 1841, 709.
DYAKS. See Borneo, vol i., p. 839.
DYAMBILIA, a town of Senegambia, 30 m. WSW of Timbu.

DYAMBILUYA, or IAMBILU, a frontier-town on the SW side of Futa, in W Africa, inhabited by Herimanki, or pastoral Fulahs, who conduct a considerable trade in cattle and butter. The town is surrounded by fields in a high state of cultivation, and gardens fenced with fig-nut hedges.
DYAN, a village of Ireland, in co. Tyrone, 7 m. S of Dungannon.
DYBERRY, a township in Wayne co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U. S. Pop. 691.
DYCE, a parish of Aberdeenshire, lying along the river Don, which separates it from Fintry. Area 4,667 acres. Pop. in 1801, 347; in 1841, 472. Granite is largely quarried in this p.

DYE, a rivulet in the p. of Strichan, in Aberdeenshire, a tributary of the Dee.-Also a small stream in Berwickshire, which, descending from the DYL Lammermoor range, flows past the v. of Longformacus, and falls into the Whitadder.
DYER, a county in the W part of the state of Tennessee, U. S., bounded by the Mississippi on the W. Area 840 sq. m. Pop. 4,484.
DYERSBURG, the cap. of Dyer co., in the state of Tennessee, U. S., 161 m. W of Nashville.
DYER'S CAPE, a cape on the E coast of North America, in N lat. 66° 40'.
DYER'S CREEK, a river of the state of New Jersey, U. S., which runs into Delaware bay, in N lat. 39° 8'.

DYFFRYN, a parcel, united to Blaine, in the p. of Llan-gynidder, co. of Brecon, 7 m. W by S of Crickhowel, Pop. in 1801, 349; in 1841, 123.— Also a hamlet in the p. of Llanthetty, co. of Brecon, 12 m. SE of Brecon. Pop. in 1801, 177; in 1841, 196. -Also a hamlet in the p. of Vainor, co. of Brecon, 14 m. S of Brecon. Pop. in 1801, 177; in 1841, 113.
DYFFRYN-CLYDACH, a hamlet in the p. of Cadoxton, co. of Glamorgan, 2 m. NW of Neath. Pop. in 1801, 726; in 1841, 1,085.
DYFFRYN-HONDDU (UPPER), a hamlet in the p. of Merthyr-Cynog, co. of Brecon, 3 m. NNE of Merthyr-Cynog, near the source of the Usk. Pop. in 1801, 193; in 1841, 206.
DYFFRYN-HONDDU (Lower), a chapelry in Merthyr-Cynog p., in the co. of Brecon. Pop. in 1801, 211; in 1841, 206.
DYFI. See Dovy.
DYG. See DEEG.
DYHONG. See DIHONG.
DYHRENFURT, a small town of Prussian Silesia, 18 m. NW of Breslau, on the Oder. Pop. 1,271.
DYHUL, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Candeish, 70 m. E by S of Surat.
DYKE, a hamlet in the p. of Bourn, Lincolnshire, 2½ m. NNE of Bourn. Pop. 241.

DYKE AND MOY, two united parishes in the co. of Elgin, excepting part of Moy which is in the shire of Nairn. The united parish extends up the Moray frith, 6 m. along the shore, and stretches S from the coast nearly the same length. Along the coast lie the Culbin or Mavistone sand-hills, which stretch through the ps. of Auldearn, Dyke, and Kinloss, on both sides, and round the mouth of the Findhorn. Boece represents these as produced by the same inundation of the sea which swept away the princely estate of Earl Godwin in Kent, in 1,100. The sea, or rather the sand, appears to be still encroaching considerably on this coast. The barony of Culbin-one of the most valuable estates in Moray—was literally buried under the sandy inundation to the depth of several feet, between the years 1670 and 1695, and still remains covered up, the only traces of its former existence being the occasional appearance of the ruins of houses. The entrance of the Findhorn into the sea has gradually moved to its present situation from its original embouchure 2 m. to the W; and on the spot where once stood the ancient town and harbour of Findhorn, nothing now appears but a stretch of sand and benty grass, affording meagre pasturage to a few sheep. The heath of Hardmoor, which adjoins the now sterile district of Culbin, celebrated as the place in which Macbeth was met by the weird sisters, while he journeyed with Banquo from the Western islands to meet King Duncan at the castle of Forres, is a bleak and barren enough heath, and its 'blasted' aspect well befits the imaginary scene of such a supernatural meeting. There are three villages in this p., - Dyke, Broom of Moy, and Kintesk. Pop. of the united p., in 1801, 1,492; in 1831, 1,451; in 1841, 1,366.

DYLE, a small but navigable river of Belgium, which takes its rise near Houtain in Brabant; and, ] after passing by Louvain and Mechlin, unites itself a little below the latter town with the Nethe, and assumes the name of the Rupel. It is navigable from Weichter downwards. This river gave name to a dep. of the Netherlands when that country was subject to France.
DYLTA, a village of Sweden, in the district and 9 m. N of Œrebro.
DYMCHURCH, a parish in Kent, 4 m. NE by N of New Romney. Area 1,210 acres. Pop. 613.
DYMOCK, a parish in the co. of Gloucester, 4 m. NNW of Newent. Area 6,270 acres. Pop 1,776.
DYNAPUR. See DINAJPUR.
DYNHYNLLE-ISSA, a township in the p. of Ruabon, Denbighshire, 4 m. SSW of Wrexham. Pop. 312.
DYNHYNLLE-UCHA, a township in the p. of Ruabon, Denbighshire. Pop. 1,027.
DYNISH, a small island near the W coast of co. Galway, at the entrance of Kilkerran bay.
DYNOW, a small town of Austrian Galicia, in the circle of Sanok, on the l. bank of the San, 25 m. W of Przemysl.
DYO, a commune of France, in the dep. of Saoneet-Loire, cant. of La Clayette. Pop. 1,036.

DYSART, a parish in Fifeshire, on the frith of Forth. Area 3,054 Scots acres, the whole of which are arable, with the exception of about 400 acres under wood. Coals have from a very early period been wrought in this p.; and the workings have been repeatedly on fire, owing, it is supposed, to the spontaneous combustion of pyrites. George Agricola, the great metallurgist, who died in 1555, takes notice of this phenomenon as occurring here. There are beds of ironstone below the coal, which are wrought where they come near the surface. The principal manufacture in the p. at present is that of checks and ticks. Besides the burgh of D., there are several populous villages in this p. Pathhead, at the SW extremity of the p., has a pop., with the adjoining v. of Sinclairtown, of 2,946. To the NE of Sinclairtown are E. and W. Gallatown, with a pop. of 1,198. There are also two smaller hamlets, viz., Boreland, containing 193 inhabitants; and Hackley-moor, containing 490.—The pop. of the parish, in 1755, was 2,367; in 1801, it was 5,385; in 1841, 7,591.—The town of D. is a royal burgh, and joins with Kirkcaldy, Kinghorn, and Burntisland in sending a member to parliament. Constituency of the parl. borough—which in 1841 had a pop. of 7,057—in 1839, 130; in 1847, 168. The town consists of three narrow streets, having a kind of square in the centre. The harbour, though not deficient in size for the trade, was formerly very unsafe; a few years ago, however, an adjoining quarry was converted into a wet dock, which has 18 ft. of water, and is sufficient to contain 17 or 18 vessels of small burden, exclusive of the old or outer harbour. The pop. of the burgh, in 1831, was 1801; in 1841, 1,885. D. formerly gave the title of Earl to the family of Murray.

DYSERT, a parish, 61 m. SW of Listowel, in DZO co. Kerry. Area 6,149 acres. Pop. in 1831, 1,230; in 1841, 1,295.—Also a p., 1½ m. S of Castle-Island, in co. Kerry. Area 6,074 acres. Pop. in 1831, 1,431; in 1841, 1,529.—Also a p., 2 m. S of Castlecomer, in co. Kilkenny. Area 7,938 acres. Pop. in 1831, 2,230; in 1841, 2,367.—Also a p., <math>1\frac{3}{4}</math> m. E of Dunleer, co. Louth. Area 1,912 acres. Pop. in 1831, 669; in 1841, 608.—Also a p., in the co. of Waterford, 4 m. E of Clonmel. Area 5,396 acres. Pop. in 1831, 1,444; in 1841, 1,406. The surface is part of the beautiful vale of the Suir.—Also a p., 5 m. SW by S of Mullingar, in co. Westmeath. Area 7,415 acres. Pop. in 1841, 1,129. The highest ground has an alt. of 320 ft.—Also a p., 31 m. SSE of Adare, co. Limerick. Area 910 acres. Pop. in 1831, 180; in 1841, 170.—Also a p., 5 m. SSE of Mount-Talbot, in co. Roscommon. Area 6,569 acres. Pop. in 1831, 1,661; in 1841, 1,739.—Also a p., 13 m. S by E of Corrofin, in co. Clare. Area 7,250 acres. Pop. in 1831, 1,666; in 1841, 1,933.
DYSERTENOS, a parish, 3 m. WSW of Stradbally, in Queen's co. Area 6,096 acres. Pop. in 1831, 1,354; in 1841, 1,415.

DYSERT-GALLEN, a parish in Queen's co., containing the town of Ballinakill. Area 10,781 acres. Pop. in 1831, 4,014; in 1841, 4,342. The two chief summits have an alt. above sea-level of respectively 869 and 1,001 ft.
DYSERTH, a parish in Flintshire, 3 m. S of St. Asaph. Pop. 892.

DYSERTMOON, or DYSERTMORE, a parish 4 m. S by E of Innistiogue, in co. Kilkenny, Leinster. Area 6,207 acres. Pop. in 1831, 2,005; in 1841, 2,123. The two chief summits have an alt. above sea-level of 308 and 524 ft.
DYUR, a village and ferry on the r. bank of the Sutledge in N. India, below Simla, and 3 days from Mundi.
DZAR-GURBAN, a river of Russian Tartary, which runs into the Irtisch, 20 m. E of Semipalatnoi.
DZARA, a village of Siberia, in the gov. of Irkutsk, in N lat. 56° 4'.
DZIALOSZYCE, a town of Poland, in the palatinate and 28 m. NE of Cracow, on the r. bank of the Warta. Pop. 3,193.
DZIALOSZYN, a town of Poland, in the prov. and 57 m. SSE of Kalisch, on the r. bank of the Warta. Pop. 1,100.
DZIDZA, a town of Albania, situated partly on the top, and partly on the declivity of a barren mountain, 18 m. E of Delvinaki.
DZIERKOWICE, a town of Poland, 5 m. SW of Urzedow.
DZIL-KARNAIM, a town of Asiatic Turkey, in the prov. and 26 m. N of Diarbekir.
DZISNA. See DISNA.
DZITOVO, a town of Russian Poland, in the gov. of Podolia, 70 m. E of Kamienetz. Pop. 1,500.
DZIUMATI, a town of Guriel, in the gov. of Caucasus, 10 m. ESE of Puti.
DZONMUREN, a river of Siberia, which falls into the Angara, 24 m. N of Irkutsk.

DZOUNGARIA. See Sungaria. about the constitution of the contract of the contract of the contract of the contract of the contract of the contract of the contract of the contract of the contract of the contract of the contract of the contract of the E EA, a lake in co. Donegal, 8 m. N of Donegal, which discharges itself by the Awin-Ea into the bay of Lochrusmore.
EACHAIG, a small river of Argyleshire, in the district of Cowal, which descends from Loch-Eck into the Holy Loch.
EACHWICK, a township in the p. of Heddonon-the-Wall, Northumberland, 91 m. WNW of Newcastle-upon-Tyne, on the Pont. Area 200 acres. Pop. 101.
EAGERNESS, a promontory on the E coast of Wigtonshire, which, forming the E side of Garlieston bay, is about a mile in length, and 3 of a mile in average breadth.
EAGLE, a parish in Lincolnshire, 73 m. WSW of Lincoln. Area 2,450 acres. Pop. 466.

EAGLE, a township of Alleghany co., in the state of New York, U. S., 264 m. SW of Albany, drained by Caneadea and Wiskoy creeks. It has an undulating surface, and is to a great extent covered with timber. The soil consists of argillaceous loam. Pop. in 1840, 1,187.—Also a township of Hancock co., in the state of Ohio, 83 m. NW of Columbus. Pop. 524.—Also a township of Browne co., in the same state. Pop. 891. The village, called Fincastle, is 17 m. from West Union.—Also a township of Ross co., in the same state. Pop. 411.
EAGLE (CAPE), a promontory on the W coast of the island of Anticosti, on the gulf of St. Lawrence, on the E side of Ellis bay.
EAGLE CREEK, a village of Gallatin co., in the state of Illinois, U.S., 102 m. SSE of Springfield, on a creek of the same name, an affluent of Saline creek.

EAGLE-HARBOUR, a village of Gaines township, Orleans co., in the state of New York, U. S., 256 m. NW of Albany. Pop. in 1840, 600.—Also a harbour in Long Island, in the gulf of St. Lawrence, 21 m. NNE from the Fox islands.

EAGLE-ISLAND, an island of co. Mayo, 4 or 5 furlongs distant from the nearest point of the mainland, 2 m. NE of Portnafranka harbour, and 3 m. WSW of Erris-head, in N lat. 54° 7', W long. 10° 6'. Two lights, one bearing E by N 395 ft. from the other, are exhibited on this island.
EAGLE-ISLAND, a low wooded island near the NE coast of Australia, between Lizard island and Cape Flattery, in S lat. 14° 42′ 20″, and E long. 145° 18′ 30″.

EAGLE (Mount), a lofty summit of co. Kerry, on the N side of the entrance of Dingle-bay.—Also one of the highest summits of the Mourne Alps, co. Down, 2,084 ft. above sea-level. It gives rise to the river Bann.

EAGLESCLIFFE, or EGGLESCLIFFE, a parish ling. and township in the co. of Durham, 12 m. NNE of Yarm, bounded on the E and S by the Tees, which is here navigable, and is crossed by a cast-iron bridge, from which a branch line extends to the Darlington and Stockton railway. Area 3,970 acres. Pop. 628. Area of township, 1,580 acres. Pop. 443.
EAGLESFIELD, a township in the p. of Brigham, Cumberland, 23 m. SW of Cockermouth. Pop. 371.

EAGLESHAM, a parish and village of Renfrew- EAR shire, 9 m. SE of Paisley. Area 15,450 acres. Pop. in 1801, 1,176; in 1841, 2,428, of whom 1,801 were in the v. The surface of this p. attains, in Balageich and Dunwan, an alt. of about 1,000 ft. above sea-level.

EAGLESHAY, one of the Shetland isles, in Islesburgh cove, to the E of St. Magnus bay, and comprised in the p. of North-Maven.-Also one of the Orkney islands, about a mile to the E of the island of Ronsay, from which it is separated by Howa sound. It is about 21 m. in length, and 1 m. broad.

EAGLE'S NEST, a lofty summit of co. Kerry, 4 m. SW of Killarney, on the l. side of the stream which connects the Upper and Middle lakes of Killarney. The literal nest of the eagle is placed in a hanging crag, near the summit of a pyramidal and almost perpendicular rock, nearly 1,300 ft. in height.
EAKLEY-LANES, a hamlet in the p. of Stoke- Goldington, Bucks. Pop. 101.
EAKRING, a parish of Nottinghamshire, 4 m. SSE of Allerton. Area 2,240 acres. Pop. 661.
EALAN-NA-ROAN, or Island of Seals, an island near the N coast of Sutherlandshire, included in the p. of Tongue. It is about 2 m. in circumf., and contains 4 or 5 families.
EALAND, a hamlet in the p. of Crowle, Lincolnshire. Pop. 167.
EALING, a parish in Middlesex, 10 m. W of St. Paul's cathedral, and near the Great Western railway. Area 3,930 acres. Pop. 8,407. It contains the hamlet of Little Ealing. Pop. 129.

EAMONT, a river of Cumberland, which has its principal source in Kirkstone hill, Westmoreland, runs through Patterdale to Ullswater, and thence to Pooly-bridge, dividing the cos. of Cumberland and Westmoreland in its course, and falls into the Eden.
EANCE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Ille-et-Vilaine, cant. of La Guerche. Pop. 1,179.
EARDINGTON, a township in the p. of Quatford, Salop, 2 m. S of Bridgenorth, and W of the Severn. Area 1,200 acres. Pop. 349.
EARDISLAND, a parish of Herefordshire, 5 m. W of Leominster, on the Arrow. Area 4,170 acres. Pop. 856.
EARDISLEY, a parish of Herefordshire, 5 m. SE of Kington, N of the Wye, and at the junction of the Hay and Kington railways. Area 4,460 acres. Pop. 756.
EARDISTON, a township in the p. of Ruyton-ofthe-Eleven-Towns, Salop. Pop. 160.
EARDLEY-END, a township in the p. of Audley, Staffordshire, 5 m. NW of Newcastle. Pop. 147.
EARIN'S HEAD, a summit of New South Wales, in the co. of Roxburgh, to the W of Mount Stir-
EARITH, a chapelry in the p. of Bluntisham, Huntingdonshire. Area 1,000 acres. Pop. 717.

EARL, a township of Lancaster co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U. S., 13 m. NW of Lancaster. It has an undulating surface, and is drained by Conestoga and Muddy creeks. Its soil consists of red shale and gravel. Pop. in 1840, 2,982. - Also a township of Berks co., in the same state, drained by Manatawny creek. It is intersected by South mountain, and is generally hilly. Pop. 1,158.
EARL, or YEARD HILL, a township of Doddington p., Northumberland. Pop. 80.
EARL-SHILTON, a chapelry in the p. of Kirkby Mallory, Leicestershire, 4 m. NE of Hinckley. Area 920 acres. Pop. 2,220.
EARL-SOHAM, a parish of Suffolk. Area 2,240 acres. Pop. 741.
EARLAS, a township in the p. of Gresford, Denbighshire, 5 m. N of Wrexham. Pop. 74.
EARL'S BARTON, a parish of Northamptonshire. Area 1,760 acres. Pop. 1,079.
EARLSFERRY, a town in the p. of Kilconquhar, Fifeshire, on the firth of Forth, 6 m. E of Largo. Pop. 496. It was an ancient royal burgh, and still retains some of its privileges.
EARLSTOKE, a parish in Wilts, 3½ m. W of E. Lavington. Area 2,400 acres. Pop. 381.

EARLSTON, a parish and village in the co. of Berwick. Pop. of p. in 1801, 1,478; in 1841, 1,756. The v. of E. stands on the banks of the Leader, 7 m. S of Lauder. The inhabitants, about 900 in number, are chiefly weavers and agricultural labourers. The fabrics woven are blankets, plaidings, and stout ginghams. E. is not a little famous as the birth-place of Thomas the Rhymer, the earliest poet of Scotland, who flourished during the latter half of the 13th cent. He is often called Thomas of Ersildun, or Ercildoune; and in his native place, as well as throughout Scotland, is celebrated among the lower orders solely on account of his reputed character of a prophet, and in connection with a few rhyming distichs-often of doubtful meaning, and apparently of multitudinous origin-which float on the tide of tradition, and along the currents of ancient and legendary literature.—Also a village in the p. of Tillicoultry, in Clackmannanshire.
EARLSTOWN, a parish in co. Kilkenny, <math>2\frac{3}{4}</math> m. E of Callan. Area 2,938 acres. Pop. in 1831, 679; in 1841, 527.
EARLSVILLE, a village in Madison co., in the state of New York, 98 m. W by N of Albany, on Chenango river.
EARLVILLE, a village in Berks co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U. S., 65 m. E of Harrisburg.— Also a v. in S. Carolina, 178 m. NW of Columbia.
EARL (West), a township in Lancaster co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U.S., 13 m. NW of Lancaster. Pop. 1,723.
EARLY, a county in the SW of the state of Georgia, U. S., watered by the Chattahoochee river. Area 128 sq. m. Pop. 5,444.

EARN, a river in Perthshire, which rises about 4 m. above the v. of Comrie, in the loch of the same name, and flowing E, in a course full of beautiful curves, falls into the Tay near the v. of Caerpow, in the p. of Abernethy. The course of the E. is considerably more than 30 m. Its principal tributary waters are the Ruchill and Lednoch, which join it at Comrie; and the Moy, which falls into it in the p. of Forteviot. It is navigable for about 3 m. above its mouth, or as far as to the Bridge-of-Earn, for vessels of from 30 to 50 tons burden.

EARN (BRIDGE OF), a village, situated on the S bank of the Earn, in the p. of Dunbarnie, 4 m. SE of Perth, at the point where the high road from Edinburgh to Perth crosses the Earn. It is completely modern, and owes its origin and increase to the vicinity of the mineral well of Pitcaithly. The Edinburgh and Perth railway has a station here 41 m. from Edinburgh, and 4 from Perth.

EARN (Loch), a beautiful sheet of water in the SW of Perthshire, 13 m. from Callander, and about 30 m. W of Perth. In length it is 7 m.; its circumf. about 19 m.; and its central depth is said to be 100 faths. "Its style is that of a lake of far greater EAS dimensions: the hills which bound it being lofty and bold and rugged, with a variety of character not found in many of even far greater magnitude and extent." [Macculloch.] Benvoirlich is the loftiest of those mountains which lend their grandeur to the scenery of Loch E.—At the E end of the lake stands the little v. of St. Fillans.
EARNLY, a parish of Sussex, 6 m. SSW of Chichester. Area 1,120 acres. Pop. 139.
EARNSHILL, a parish of Somersetshire, 4 m. SSW of Langport. Area 390 acres. Pop. in 1801, 20; in 1841, 12.

EARSDON, a parish in Northumberland, 4 m. NW by N of North Shields. It contains the townships of Brierdean, Blackworth, Blyth, South and Newsham, E., Hartley, Holywell, Seaton-Delaval, and Sighill. Area of p. 11,060 acres. Pop. in 1801, 3,451; in 1841, 9,429. Pop. of the township, in 1831, 628.—Also a township in the p. of Hebburn, Northumberland, 5 m. N of Morpeth. Pop. in 1841, 683.
EARSHAM, a parish in Norfolk, 1 m. W by S of Bungay. Area 2,960 acres. Pop. in 1801, 658; in 1841, 731.
EARTHAM, a parish in Sussex, 53 m. NE by E of Chichester. Area 2,110 acres. Pop. in 1801, 114; in 1841, 117.

EASBY, a parish in the N. R. of Yorkshire. It comprises the townships of Aske, Brompton-upon- Swale, E., and Skeeby. Area 5,090 acres. Pop. in 1801, 693; in 1841, 771.—Also a township in the p. of Stokesley, N. R. of Yorkshire, 4 m. E by N of Stokesley. Pop. in 1801, 138; in 1841, 144.—Also a township in the p. of Brampton, Cumberland, 10 m. NE by E of Carlisle. Pop. in 1801, 135; in 1841, 84.

EASDALE, an island of the Hebrides, in the p. of Kilbrandon. It is about 1 m. in diam., nearly circular, and consists chiefly of slate, which is traversed in several places with basaltic veins, and thin layers of quartzose and calcareous stones. Slate has been quarried here upwards of 150 years, and of late has been wrought to a great extent. The number of workmen employed in 1795 was about 300; and the number of slates sold in that year was 5,000,000, at 25s. per 1,000. They now fetch in Glasgow £3 per 1,000 of the largest size, and 45s. for the smaller size. The constant demand for the slate has caused the surface to be cut very low, except at the S end; and the greater part is now on a level with the sea.
EASEBOURNE, a parish in Sussex, 1 m. NE of Midhurst, on the N bank of the Rother. Area 5,680 acres. Pop. in 1801, 1,207; in 1841, 1,708.
EASENHALL, a hamlet in the p. of Monks- Kirby, co. of Warwick, 4 m. NW of Rugby. Pop. in 1801, 115; in 1841, 214.

EASINGTON, a parish in the co.-palatine of Durham, 9½ m. E by N of Durham; comprising the townships of Haswell, E., Hawthorn, and Shotton. Area 12,400 acres. Pop. in 1801, 944; in 1841, 5,573. -The town of E., which is of considerable extent, occupies an elevated situation near the sea. Pop. in 1801, 487; in 1831, 693; in 1841, 812.—Also a township in the p. of Belford, Northumberland, 13 m. NE of Belford. Pop. in 1801, 151; in 1841, 180. —Also a parish in the co. of Oxford, 4 m. SW by S of Tetsworth. Area 380 acres. Pop. in 1801, 31; in 1841, 24.—Also a parish in the E. R. of Yorkshire, 6 m. SE of Patrington, on the coast, containing the townships of E. and Out-Newton. Area 3,020 acres. Pop. in 1801, 341; in 1841, 546.—Also a parish in the N.R. of Yorkshire, 9 m. E by N of Guisborough. Area 3,856 acres. Pop. in 1801, 500; in 1841, 791.—Also a township in Slaidburn p., in the W. R. of Yorkshire, 7 m. NNW of Clitheroe. Area 11,740 acres. Pop. in 1801, 376; in 1841, 409.

EASINGWOLD, a market-town and parish in the N. R. of Yorkshire, and 13 m. NNW of York. The town is irregularly built; the environs are flat; in the vicinity are some chalybeate springs. Area 10,070 acres. Pop. in 1801, 1,805; in 1841, 2,171.

EASKEY, a parish and village in co. Sligo. Area of p. 13,285 acres. Pop. 6,349.—The v. is 111 m. NNE of Ballina. Pop. in 1831, 289; in 1841, 421. -Also a rivulet in co. Sligo, issuing from Lough Esk, and flowing 95 m. N to the ocean. * EAST, most names with this prefix will be found under the second word of the compound term. A few omitted articles are inserted here.
EAST, a township in Carroll co., in the state of Ohio, U. S. Pop. 995.
EAST BAY, a harbour in Queen Charlotte's sound, in New Zealand, formed by the island of Arapaoa, and consisting of three principal arms. At the head is the v. of Mokupeka.
EAST BETHLEHEM, a township of Washington co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U.S., 197 m. W of Harrisburg, skirted by the Monongahela river. Pop. 2,312.
EAST BLOOMFIELD, a township in Ontario co., in the state of New York, U.S., 8 m. W of Canandaigua. Pop. 1,986.—Also a v. in Bloomfield township, Crawford co., Pennsylvania.
EAST BOTHNIA. See BOTHNIA.

EASTBOURNE, a parish in Sussex. Area 5,850 acres. Pop. in 1801, 1,668; in 1841, 3,015. The village of E. is 6 m. S of Hailsham. It consists of 4 straggling divisions. It has lately become a fashionable bathing-place, and has a chalybeate spring of the same properties as the Bristol waters. The coast of Sussex has long been gradually suffering from the encroachments of the sea; and at E. it is protected by strong groins or frames of wood-work and shingle. A little to the S is Beachey-head, the highest headland on all the S coast.
